policy_statement: Promote, conserve and value animal, plant and forest genetic heritage to increase resilience to climate change (including resistance to drought, diseases and pests).
mechanism_description: Promote, conserve and value animal, plant and forest genetic heritage so ecosystems and production systems are more resilient to climate change impacts such as drought, diseases and pests.
primary_causal_pathway: Climate hazard (drought/pests/disease) → genetic diversity conservation/use → ↑ system robustness/adaptive capacity → ↓ climate impact damages (yield loss, dieback, mortality)
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Change in human activity/system: Land and biodiversity governance prioritizes conserving, restoring, and using diverse animal/plant/forest genetic resources (e.g., local breeds, seed varieties, provenance selection in forestry) in managed and semi-natural systems.  
2) Mediating physical/ecological process: Higher genetic diversity increases functional trait variation and adaptive potential, improving tolerance/resistance to heat and water stress and reducing susceptibility/spread of pests and pathogens; it also supports recovery after extreme events.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Reduced vulnerability and losses under climate change (more stable yields, lower livestock/forest mortality, maintained ecosystem functioning) and improved capacity to cope with increasing drought and biotic stress.
dominant_pathway_test: Conserving and deploying genetic diversity primarily increases ecosystem and agricultural resilience to climate hazards (drought, pests, disease), reducing climate-impact damages rather than directly reducing GHG emissions.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: The causal chain is “climate stressors → resilient genetics → reduced damages,” i.e., decreasing exposure/sensitivity and increasing adaptive capacity to drought, pests, and disease.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: The biggest climate-relevant effect is not “lower emissions” (Mitigation) nor “same service with less input” (Resource Efficiency); it is resilience to climate impacts. Nature-based framing may apply in some instances, but the defining lever here is genetic diversity as an adaptation strategy rather than ecosystem carbon sequestration.  
3) Edge cases applied: Some genetic conservation actions (especially in forests) can indirectly protect carbon stocks by preventing dieback, and some biodiversity programs may be counted as nature-based; however, those are secondary co-benefits relative to the stated primary purpose of climate resilience.  
4) Applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: Any policy whose mechanism is conserving/valuing genetic heritage to improve resistance to drought/pests/disease is fundamentally an adaptation mechanism because it targets vulnerability to climate hazards.  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Any finer labeling (e.g., “ecosystem-based adaptation,” “agricultural adaptation,” “forest health,” “biodiversity conservation”) depends on policy-specific details and is not assigned at the mechanism level.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If a policy emphasizes conserving genetic resources primarily to maintain or increase long-term carbon sinks (e.g., selecting genotypes specifically to maximize sequestration), it could blur toward Mitigation/Nature-Based Solutions; under this canonical mechanism as stated (resilience to drought/pests/disease), Adaptation remains primary.
key_indicators: "Promote, conserve and value"; "genetic heritage"; "increase resilience to climate change"; "resistance to drought, diseases and pests"
co_benefits: Biodiversity conservation and ecosystem health; potential mitigation co-benefits via healthier forests/carbon storage; cultural/heritage value and rural livelihoods
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be implemented as an incentive program or regulatory protection in practice, but the text alone is framed as an objective/strategy without a specified funding, mandate, or standard.
